title: Classical simulation of free-fermionic dynamics and quantum chemistry with magic input
authors: Changhun Oh, Michał Oszmaniec, Oliver Reardon-Smith, Zoltán Zimborás
abstract: Establishing the precise computational boundary between classically tractable fermionic systems and those capable of genuine quantum advantage is a central challenge in quantum simulation. While injecting non-Gaussian ``magic" inputs into free-fermion circuits is widely expected to generate intractable complexity, we identify a physically motivated intermediate regime. Supported by rigorous bounds and numerical evidence, we show that for a class of paired non-Gaussian fermionic states, essential quantum simulation primitives -- transition amplitudes, overlaps, and arbitrary-weight number correlators -- can be efficiently approximated to additive error under free-fermionic dynamics. This tractability stems from an algebraic reduction that compresses exponentially large multiparticle interference into a single coefficient of a multivariate Pfaffian polynomial. Because these classical estimators match the intrinsic $O(1/\sqrt{K})$ statistical uncertainty of quantum hardware utilizing $K$ measurement shots, they constitute a practical benchmark. Building on this foundation, we construct an additive-error estimator for high-weight Wilson observables in the noninteracting quench of recent trapped-ion experiments, providing a rigorous classical benchmark. Extending this to quantum chemistry, we demonstrate that core overlap-based subroutines for antisymmetrized products of strongly orthogonal geminals admit exact Pfaffian reductions. Ultimately, these results sharpen the boundary of quantum advantage, establishing that the paired-electron scaffold is effectively dequantized and clarifying exactly where quantum resources are indispensable.
summary: This paper addresses the challenge of simulating fermionic systems that use non-Gaussian 'magic' inputs, which are typically computationally hard. The authors demonstrate that for a specific class of paired-electron states, complex many-body dynamics can be efficiently simulated on classical computers using a new Pfaffian-based method. This approach allows for the efficient estimation of key observables like transition amplitudes and number correlators with additive error. The results establish a practical classical benchmark for recent trapped-ion quantum simulation experiments and help define the limits of quantum advantage in fermionic systems.
main_problem: Determining the computational boundary between classically tractable and intractable fermionic systems when using non-Gaussian 'magic' inputs in free-fermion circuits.
main_result: The authors identify a tractable intermediate regime where essential simulation primitives for paired non-Gaussian states can be efficiently approximated to additive error using a mixed-Pfaffian reduction.
method: An algebraic reduction technique that compresses multi-particle interference into a single coefficient of a multivariate Pfaffian polynomial, combined with a randomized Monte Carlo estimator using discrete Fourier filtering.
model_or_system: Fermionic systems involving number-preserving Gaussian evolution (passive FLO) acting on structured non-Gaussian inputs, specifically block-product paired states and Antisymmetric Pair-Slot Gaussian (APSG) states.
key_observables: Transition amplitudes, overlaps, arbitrary-weight number correlators, transition reduced density matrices (RDMs), and high-weight Wilson-loop observables.
important_parameters: Additive error epsilon, failure probability delta, number of measurement shots K, and the particle-number-preserving nature of the evolution.
main_assumptions: The efficiency is proven for a specific class of paired non-Gaussian states; the results focus on additive-error estimation rather than exact calculation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the workflow from magic inputs through FLO to Pfaffian reduction; Figure 2 illustrates the sampling process; Figure 3 visualizes the mixed-Pfaffian extraction as a colored perfect matching problem; Figure 5 shows RMSE scaling.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of non-Gaussian complexity, presents the mixed-Pfaffian algebraic reduction, proves theorems for transition amplitudes and overlaps, extends the framework to quantum chemistry subroutines and non-unitary maps, and provides benchmarks for trapped-ion experiments.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous classical benchmark for near-term fermionic quantum simulators and clarifies the precise boundary where quantum advantage becomes necessary in many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Efficient classical estimation of non-Gaussian fermionic dynamics. The schematic illustrates the evolution of a structured non-Gaussian input state—composed of n disjoint two-particle states (paired magic inputs)—under a passive fermionic linear optics (FLO) unitary ˆU, which is the main focus of this work. While such dynamics are widely expected to exhibit exponential computational complexity, our mixed-Pfaffian reduction structurally bypasses this combinatorial explosion. Supported by rigorous variance bounds and robust empirical scaling, the framework enables operationally scalable additive- error approximations. FIG. 4. Noninteracting benchmark: doublon dynamics, triplet density, and Wilson loops. Left column: low- weight observables, namely the doublon number Nd(t) and the nearest-neighbor triplet diagnostic ntriplets(t), for which our Monte Carlo results are benchmarked against exact, FLO, and experimentally reported references. Right two columns: high- weight doublon Wilson-loop observables in the two standard cuts used in Ref. [3], shown as functions of perimeter at fixed area and as functions of area at fixed perimeter, with the two time windows indicated in each panel. FIG. 5. RMSE scaling of the mixed-Pfaffian estimator for two-sided APSG inputs. We benchmark Haar-random passive FLO instances for block-product APSG states with local pair-slot number rt = r. Each row corresponds to rt = 2, 3, 4. The left column shows the empirical RMSE versus the Monte Carlo sample count K at fixed N, and the right column shows the RMSE versus N at fixed K. Curves are labeled by the left and right local uniformities (uL, uR), with (1, 1) denoting the fully uniform case. Across the tested Haar- random instances, the fully uniform point gives the largest empirical RMSE, while non-uniform deformations reduce the sampling error; the K-dependence is consistent with the...

FIG. 6. Uniformity sweep for two-sided APSG deformations. For Haar-random passive FLO instances, we fix N and K, set uL = uR = u, and plot the empirical RMSE as a function of the local uniformity u. From left to right, the panels correspond to rt = 2, 3, 4. The RMSE increases monotonically toward the fully uniform point u = 1, directly supporting the interpretation that the uniform APSG input is the empirical variance bottleneck in these benchmarks.

FIG. 7. Sample-complexity contours for real-time Hirsch HS sampling under Phasecraft-matched Strang Trotterization (k = 4, ∆t = t/k). (a) Typical-scale proxy. (b) Conservative Hoeffding-type upper bound. Stars mark (W, t) = (4, 2); here ϵ = 0.01, δ = 0.01.
FIG. 8. Sample-complexity contours for extent-based sampling under Phasecraft-matched Trotterization (k = 4, ∆t = t/k). The star marks (W, t) = (4, 2); here ϵ = 0.01, δ = 0.01.
